fixtures.whyWatch: The opening match which is also a repeat of the opening match of the 2010 edition (a 1-1 draw then). Not much too say other than that the atmosphere in Estadio Azteca should be phenomenal.

Neither team are fancied to go far, but to make it out of the group a win in the first game would be ideal, especially with South Korea facing the much-fancied and well-supported host, Mexico, in the second game.

Canada had a disappointing campaign last time around in Qatar, losing all three of their games. They are desperate to start on the right foot and look for their first win ever in the World Cup. Bosnia might not have flashy superstars, but underestimate them at your own peril, as they upset both Wales and Italy en route to the World Cup despite being unfancied.

All the talk, hype, pressure, and expectation surrounding the US would not cease unless they start with a positive result against Paraguay, making their first appearance since 2010. La Albirroja finished sixth among the 10 South American countries, but can play spoiler to the host.

After 28 years away, Scotland are making their World Cup return. They must start strong against arguably the weakest side of the group. The midfield duo of McGinn and McTominay must be in their best form for The Tartan Army to have a hope of progressing, which they haven't accomplished in their history. Haiti can spring a surprise, they are a well-drilled side able to defend resolutely. Frustrating the Scotland attack and trying to nick a goal maybe their best route here.

Turkey are making their first appearance in 24 years where they made it all the way to the semifinals en route to a third-place finish. They were considered as dark horses in Euro 2020 and 2024. They certainly did not live up to the billing in the former, losing all three group games and scoring only one goal. In the latter, they made it to the quarterfinals, and were unlucky to lose against the Netherlands. The Socceroos will not be an easy opponent as they have a strong defensive spine coupled with exciting young attackers in Mo Toure and Nestory Irakunda.

Since last winning the World Cup for a record fifth time in 2002, Brazil has been knocked out in the quarterfinals four times. Their only semifinal appearance in 2014 has its own Wikipedia entry (for all the wrong reasons). Carlo Ancelotti will be expected to bring his trademark man-management and break their World Cup hoodoo. Morocco has been on a rise since becoming the first African-Arab nation to make the World Cup semifinal in 2022. They've won the African Cup of Nations (scratch that, awarded the trophy) and will not cower against the Selecao.

Qatar is making their second straight appearance where they hoped to fare better than last time where home pressure seemed to get to them. Switzerland are making their sixth straight appearance at the World Cup, where they've made it out of the group stage every time except in 2010 (where ironically they dealt Spain's only loss in their successful campaign).

Ivory Coast will look to make it to the knockout stage for the first time in their history. To do that they will be relying on veterans such as Franck Kessie, Nicolas Pepe, and Jean-Michael Seri, along with some exciting young attackers in Simon Adingra, Amad Diallo, and Yan Diomande. Ecuador have a strong defense marshalled by Piero Hincapie and Willian Pacheco, with Moises Caicedo as the midfield general. Whoever emerge victorious will be favorite to make it to the next round.

Since winning the World Cup in 2014, Germany has suffered back-to-back elimination in the group stage. Entering this World Cup, they will be considered as one of the favorite, but a convincing start against debutant Curaço is a must. Curaço, the smallest nation by area and population to qualify for the World Cup will look to keep the game close and avoid heavy defeat.

Whisper it quietly, but Japan seem to have changed their mindset of being happy to be a perennial participant, into one that is seeking to be considered as a serious contender. A positive start against the Netherlands will only strengthen their credentials. The Netherlands are not the Netherlands of yore where Johann Cruyff, Ruud Gullit, Marco van Basten, or Dennis Bergkamp play a scintillating style of football. It remains to be seen whether a more functional approach championed by Ronald Koeman will lead to a World Cup glory for the Oranje.

The team that won none of their games in the group stage but still made it to the World Cup play-offs by virtue of  their Nations League performance, Sweden could well be a team to keep an eye on. They have two top strikers in Alexander Isak and Viktor Gyokeres, though they had contrasting fortunes last season. Gyokeres helped Arsenal won their first Premier League title in 22 years and made it all the way to the Champions League final, while Isak struggled with injuries and barely made an impact after a record breaking summer move to Liverpool. Tunisia did not concede a goal in qualifying, but this is a rather inexperienced squad, which might struggle in this stage.

Uruguay must heed the lesson of Argentina in 2022 where they lost their opening game of the group stage against Saudi Arabia, led by Salem Al-Dawsari. The Green Falcons did not convince in qualifying, their initial campaign was led by Roberto Mancini. But the Italian resigned in October 2024 after earning only five points in their first four games. Frenchman Herve Renard returned for a second spell and safely navigated the last stage of the qualification round before getting the boot in April 2026. Current coach Georgios Donis named a squad without ever competing in a competitive fixture.

Spain would be overwhelming favorite to win this match despite missing their best attacking talent in Lamine Yamal. Debutant Cape Verde has a well-settled squad, but the talent gap means damage limitation might be the order of the day.

Iran enters this World Cup with a lot of off-field issues which are beyond their control. Whether this plays into the hand of New Zealand remains to be seen, who will be relying on the fit-again Chris Wood to drive them past the group stage for the first time ever.

The so-called Golden Generation is no more, with Thibaut Courtois, Kevin De Bruyne, Romelu Lukaku, and Axel Witsel the only remaining members of the 2014 World Cup squad. This is a team in transition, and now is the time for the likes of Jeremy Doku, Charles De Ketelaere, and Alexis Saelemakers to make their mark on this team. Egypt will rely on the brilliance of Mohamed Salah, who will be determined to improve upon their last showing, a winless campaign in 2018.

Perhaps the most intriguing matchup among the opening group matches, Senegal, then making their World Cup debut, shocked the defending champion back in 2002 in the tournament's first game thanks to a goal from the late Papa Bouba Diop. The French imploded afterwards as injury took their toll en route to a group stage exit. A Senegal win now would not be considered as much as an upset as 24 years ago, with the Lions of Teranga possessing multiple stars that plays in Europe's top five leagues.

This World Cup marks Norway's first appearance in a major tournament since Euro 2000. There is a hint of Golden Generation surroinding Norway, which could be a blessing and a curse. Erling Haaland and Martin Odegaard will be their key players, with the latter memorably scoring nine goals in an U-20 World Cup fixtures back in 2019. Iraq are no pushover though, showing mental resilience and strength to qualify for the World Cup despite 29 hours of journey by bus and plane to make it to Mexico for the playoffs where they prevailed 2-1 over Bolivia to cap off a 21 game qualifying odyssey.

The defending champs will begin their title defense against Algeria, who qualified easily for this tournament. There are several European-based talents in their starting XI, which will be led by 35-years old Riyad Mahrez. Argentina would be looking to avoid a shock in the opening game like in 2022, although it did turned out well for them in the end.

Austria are a more expansive squad these days, a well-drilled high-pressing machine under the guidance of Ralf Rangnick. Their squad may lack true star power, but they will fancy their chance against debutant Jordan.

A strong, positive start will be the aim for both nations, as they will face tougher opponents in their next two games. Antoine Semenyo will lead the attack along with the experienced Inaki Williams and the speedster Kamaldeen Sulemana. Panama is a well-organized squad and have plenty of experience and physicality. They will look to build on strong showings from the 2023 Gold Cup and the 2024 Copa America.
The tireless and ageless Luka Modric aiming for one last shot of glory against an England side aiming to end 60 years of hurt. England are not short on stars but can they start off on the right foot?

The last time DR Congo made the World Cup, they were still called Zaire. The Leopards have a strong, well-organized defense, but lacking in attack despite boasting experienced players in Cedric Bakambu and Yoanne Wissa. The latter will look to put a nightmare season behind him, where he struggled because of injuries and lack of form with Newcastle after moving from Brentford. Portugal have a strong group of players and were the most recent Nations League winner, and with Ronaldo in the squad, anything can happen.

The debutant from Central Asia will look to cause an upset, with by a sturdy defense led by Abdukodir Khusanov, who can learn a thing or two from his coach, the 2006 World Cup winning captain of Italy, Fabio Cannavaro. Colombia should be considered as a dark horse, with Luises, Diaz and Suarez looking to start the tournament with a win.

Two teams that opened their World Cup campaign with a loss, Czezchia and South Africa must win this game to keep alive their hope of progressing to the knockout stage. Both teams saw little of the ball in their respective matches, with Czechia being more comfortable with this particular style, hitting South Korea in counters and utilizing set-pieces. Bafana Bafana must take the initiative be more expansive, something they failed to do against Mexico.

Group B remains for the taking after all countries involved drew 1-1 in their opening games. Switzerland will rue their wastefulness against Qatar, delivering 26 shots but only scoring one goal. Bosnia have shown that they can absorb pressure and hit the opponent with a sucker punch.

Canada earned their first ever point at the World Cup, and will seek to go one better by getting their first win. Canada must put away their chances as Qatar have shown that they could defend and will not go away even after going a goal down. The 2022 World Cup host is also seeking their first ever win in this stage.

This match will determine who can seize control of the group and book their ticket to the knockout stage. South Korea displayed tremendous poise by winning from behind against Czechia in their opening game. Both sides will rely on veteran attackers, with Son Heung-min leading South Korea, while El Tri will look to Raul Jimenez, who scored his first ever World Cup goal against South Africa.

Brazil needed to get their campaign going by picking up a win after being held to a draw in their first game against Morocco. Haiti showed grit in their first game defeat against Scotland. The good news for Brazil? They've never lost to an opponent from the CONCACAF region.
Scotland will seek to clinch their ticket to the knockout stage as soon as possible, but they will come up against a very impressice Morocco side, the (legally) reigning champions of Africa.

Two teams that will be desperate to win after a disappointing result in the first game. Turkey should be encouraged by their domination against Australia, but they are sorely lacking ideas in the final third. If Paraguay can weather the onslaught of attacks, they might land a sucker punch and leave with all three points.

With both teams winning their first group games, a defeat should not be terminal for their prospect of progressing through the group. However, comments from US pundits, calling Australia "average" and this match would be a "layup" have added some spice.
Germany were ruthless against Curaçao, but will face sterner test against Ivory Coast. Yan Diomande will seek to build on his impressive outing in Ivory Coast's win against Ecuador.

This is potentially an elimination game for both sides. Curaçao were impressive until they scored against Germany, which seem to have woken up their opponent. Ecuador need to show more offensively if they want to avoid an early exit from the tournament.

Sweden were impressive against a very poor Tunisia side, but they will be up against it in the form of Group F favorites, Netherlands. Alexander Isak brilliant display (two assists and one goal) will be key for Sweden. Netherlands were pegged back twice against Japan, with Ronald Koeman's subsitutions failing to generate any momentum.

Tunisia made one change already before the game, sacking Sabri Lamouchi and replacing him with Herve Renard. The Frenchman is taking part in his third World Cup and will seek to improve the team's defensive organization. They will need all the help they can get as Japan looked threatening and will be determined in getting their first win of the tournament.

With all four teams having one point each, it is all to play for in Group H. Cape Verde defensive organization against Spain, where they only committed one foul, will be studied by future generations, and they will be asked to do more of the same against Uruguay. Federico Valverde and co will need to be sharper in front of goal if they want to avoid coming unstuck just like Spain.

Will the pre-tournament favorite head for the exit? Alarm bells are ringing after Spain failed to score against Cape Verde. They will be facing more of the same against Saudi Arabia, although the Asian side have shown that they have the offensive firepower to hurt bigger nations (see Argentina at the last World Cup). The good news for Spain is that Lamine Yamal should be fit to start.

Another group where it's still wide open, Belgium will aim for all three points against an Iran side that shown a lot of grit despite off-field factors affecting their preparation. Belgium started slowly against Egypt, something that Iran can take advantage of.

A double from Elijah Just was not enough for The Kiwis as they were held to a 2-2 draw against Iran in their first game. Their quest for a first World Cup win ever continues against Egypt, also looking for their first tournament win ever. Omar Marmoush (five shots v Belgium) and Mohamed Salah (one assist, three chances created) will be the ones to watch for New Zealand.

Senegal showed that they can frustrate France even if their defense was breached in the end. But with Erling Braut Haaland in squad, Norway could book their passage to the knockout stage with a win. Sadio Mane and co must avoid defeat to have any hope of progressing from the group of death.
France was made to work for their victory, but their firepower proved to be too much in the end. Iraq will be desperate to avoid a heavy defeat like in their opening game.

Two teams that started their tournament with a win lock horns for control of the group. Argentina barely got out of second gear and dominated Algeria. They will face tougher opposition in Austria, a team that is willing to press and bring the game to the Argentinians.
Whoever loses this game will likely be knocked out of the compeititon. Algeria should have more quality to overcome Jordan, but the debutant will not be overrun as they shown against Austria.

England did not look fully-formed just yet against Croatia but still prevailed by two goals. Ghana needed a late goal to win against Panama and did not create a lot of opportunities. Expect England to get another win, even if they don't fully convinced.

Is this truly the end for Modric's generation? The 40-year old was overrun by England in the first game, but showed why they can't be taken lightly by pegging The Three Lions twice. They might just have too much for Panama in the end.

Roberto Martinez must think of a solution quickly if Portugal wants to avoid an early exit. In Uzbekistan, they will face a resolute defence, so the question is will they persevere with a legend of the game that has not scored in 10 straight games in major tournaments (and registered zero shots on target against DR Congo)?

DR Congo opened their campaign with a creditable draw against Portugal. To get a win against Colombia they will need to be better in front of goal, and a lot closer too, as they registered the most shots from outside the box (six out of eight) with only two shots on target. Colombia will look to rely on Luis Diaz once again, who recorded an assist and a goal against Uzbekistan.

A draw suit both teams, as Scotland would finish with four points and Brazil with five points. The Tartan Army would likely finish as one of the top third-placed teams. Brazil, whether they finish top or second in the group, will face a team from Group F. One thing to watch for is whether Neymar will make his much anticipated return.

Haiti is simply playing for pride, hoping to avoid a winless campaign. Morocco is in a similar situation with Brazil, they don't necessarily need to win, and they won't know which Group F team they will be facing in the next round.
Canada will be aiming to win the group which they can achieve by a win or a draw. Topping the group also means they will stay in Canada for the Round of 32 and Round of 16.

Bosnia needs a win to keep their hopes of progressing to the knockout stage. They will take a lot of encouragement from Canada's performance in the last game and knowing that Qatar will miss two of their starters in defense because of suspensions.

Mexico already clinched top spot of their group and gets to stay home for the first two games of the knockout stage should they make it that far. Czechia needs to win as a draw would only leave them with two points, which won't be enough to make it through.

South Korea is in a good position to qualify, needing only to avoid defeat, which will see them through either as a runner-up or in third place. South Africa needs to show more attacking intent than their previous two games if they are to have any chance of progressing.

Curaçao have a very slim hope of progressing after a creditable 0-0 draw against Ecuador. They will need to defeat Ivory Coast with a healthy margin (they are currently on -6 goal difference). Ivory Coast has enough attacking threat and were undone at the end by Germany, and will be determined to make it through the group for the first time in their history.

It's do or die time for Ecuador, as they need a win to avoid going out in the group stage. The good news is they will be playing a Germany side safe and sound to next stage, which might influence Julian Nagelsmann's team selection.

Two teams that knew a draw would be enough to take them through, but it's unlikely either will just play for a draw. A win for either side, depending on the result of Netherlands v Tunisia, will see them top the group and possibly landing a more favorable opponent from Group C.

Tunisia has been eliminated and is playing for pride. The Netherlands seemed to have found an attacking combination that works with the trio of Cody Gakpo, Brian Brobbey, and Crysencio Summerville (or Donyell Malen) wreaking havoc in the win against Sweden. Depending on the outcomes in other groups, the Netherlands will aim to win the group and land in the softer side of the bracket (possibly avoiding playing Group C winner, Brazil in the Round of 32 and either France or Norway in the Round of 16)
Wikipedia's definition of dead rubber: "a term used in sporting parlance to describe a match in a series where the series result has already been decided by earlier matches."

When asked about possibly playing for a draw if the score remains goalless with 10 minutes left, Socceroos defender, Jason Geria said that "It's not in our nature to play for a draw". If his words are anything to go by, expect Australia to seek a win. Paraguay must avoid defeat as their goal difference might not be good enough to see them through with three points.

Mbappe v Haaland, Olise v Odegaard, this is one of the best matchup in the group stage although with little stakes involved. Both teams are already through, the only thing to play for is top spot of the group. The winner of this group will play a third placed teams in the next round, before potentially meeting Germany in the Round of 16.

Iraq is not mathematically eliminated just yet, but they will need to win by a significant margin (they currently have a -6 goal difference) and hope for favors elsewhere. Senegal is also in the same boat, they need to win at all cost to avoid elimination.

After earning their first win in World Cup, Egyp is now poised to go through to the knockout stage, potentially as a group winner if they beat Iran. Iran could also make it through with a draw depending on result elsewhere.

A must win game for The Kiwis to progress to the knockout stage. They have played brilliantly in their first two games, but were undone by a World Class player in Mo Salah in their last game, a 3-1 defeat to Egypt. If there's something that the Belgians lack, it's not World Class players in the attacking end, even if they haven't quite get going yet.

Truly the cinderella story of the tournament so far, can Cape Verde stave off elimination and continue their dreamlike journey? Avoid defeat against Saudi Arabia and they potentially will make it to the knockout stage. They will be supported by half a million Cape Verdeans back home and neutrals everywhere in the world.

A tale of two games for Spain, they dominated proceedings against Cape Verde but couldn't find a goal. Against Saudi Arabia, they dominated proceedings and scored four past them. Uruguay will be a tough opponent, and they will need to win to be assured of a spot in the knockout stage.
England did not fully convinced in their first two games. A less-than-convincing performance against already eliminated Panama will definitely raise the alarm for those supporting The Three Lions.

Both teams are on three points, and potentially could go through with a draw. They might be keeping an eye on England's result, as a win for either side (in case England slip up), can see them leapfrog The Three Lions.

If it seems like most of the preview for the final games in the group stage starts with "Both teams know a draw is enough for them", it is an unfortunate byproduct of a 48-team tournament. That said, Austria and Algeria know that a draw is enough for them to go through.

Argentina is already through as group winner and will take on the runner-up of Group H in the next round. Will they give Messi a rest? Or will they give him more opportunities to stretch his lead as the World Cup's all-time leading goalscorer?
Colombia is assured to go through to the next round, potentially as a group winner if they avoid defeat against Portugal, who have Cristiano Ronaldo back scoring goals.

This is a game where both teams still have a chance mathematically to progress, but they might already know their fate by the time their game kicks off. Uzbekistan must win and hope results elsewhere go their way, and DR Congo needs to win.

Both sides should fancy their chances of progressing to the next round. Canada, playing away from home for the first time in this World Cup, won't be without Ismael Kone who suffered a horrendous injury against Qatar, but will probably have Alphonso Davies back.
storylines: Canada missing Kone and home field advantage

fixtures.whyWatch: Was that loss against Ecuador a sign of a team knowing they are safe and sound through to the knockout stage or is there a sign for concern? Either way, Germany will need to figure out their gameplan quickly against another South American opposition.
storylines: Is Germany for real?

fixtures.whyWatch: Only France and Germany scored as many goals as De Oranje in the group stage, but the knockout stage is a more cagey affair. Can the trio of Gakpo, Brobbey, and Malen unlock the Moroccan defense?
storylines: Three members of the Moroccan team were born in the Netherlands
Can the Netherlands continue their red-hot attack in the knockout rounds?

fixtures.whyWatch: Slowly but surely this Brazil team is kicking into gear. They will be up against a formidable opponent in Japan, who will be desperate to finally earn a win in the knockout stage of the World Cup.
storylines: Is Brazil actually good?
Japan seeking first knockout stage win in their World Cup history

fixtures.whyWatch: The only thing capable of stopping France at the World Cup has been thunderstorm. Sweden will be hoping to keep the game close for as long as possible and will be hoping that Isak, Gyokeres, and Elanga can hurt the France defense.
storylines: Who will score more goals, Dembele or Mbappe?

fixtures.whyWatch: Both teams will be confident of securing a spot in the last 16, but Norway have the added benefit of resting their starters. Haaland will be the player to watch for a Norway side that possess the fourth highest conversion rate in this tournament (22.86%).
storylines: Ivory Coast will want to continue flying the flag for Africa
Norway rested most of their starters in the last game, so will be fresh for this game

fixtures.whyWatch: Mexico will be desperate to break a 40-year drought of never winning a match in the knockout stage, with seven straight eliminations in the round of 16 between 1994 to 2018. They have benefited from a weak group and now will face a real test in Ecuador, who finally showed some signs of life in their win against Germany.
storylines: Mexico seeking a first knockout stage win since 1986
Home support advantage for El Tri
Did Ecuador finally turn a page after winning against Germany?

fixtures.whyWatch: England got the job done and qualified for the knockout stage with a straightforward win against Panama. Against DR Congo, they will be hoping to have the Declan Rice back in the starting lineup.
storylines: England faced the least amount of shots in the group stage (12)
DR Congo took the second least amount of shots (15) among teams that qualifies to the knockout stage

fixtures.whyWatch: Will USA marches on? Or will Bosnia and Herzegovina play the spoiler? Bosnia does not carry a lot of offensive threat (0.07 xG per shot), so they will be determine to keep it tight in the back before launching a counter attack.
storylines: Home support advantage
One-time US international Esmir Bajraktarević will be hoping to knockout the country of his birth

fixtures.whyWatch: Two teams that scored five goals in the last game of the group stage to progress to the Round of 32. The question will be, if that was real sign of progress or more of a case of desperation and poor oppositions?
storylines: Last hurrah for each Golden Generations? (De Bruyne, Lukaku, Mane, Koulibaly, and Gueye)

fixtures.whyWatch: It's unfair to simplify these matchup as a game between two fourty somethings in Luka Modric and Cristiano Ronaldo. But this will be someone's final World Cup game, this could sneakily be the best game of the whole round.
storylines: The Expendables: Ronaldo v Modric
Potential World Cup farewell for one legend

fixtures.whyWatch: Spain finished their group stage with a win, but it cost them two players as Yeremy Pino and Nico Williams suffered injuries. Austria were on the brink of elimination until a last-gasp goal from Sasa Kalajdzic. They will not shy away from this challenge.
storylines: Spain dealing with injuries (Pino, Williams)

fixtures.whyWatch: After drawing against Qatar 1-1 in their opening game, Switzerland found their shooting boot, putting four past Bosnia and then defeating Canada (thus sending the Canadians to LA instead of staying in Vancouver). They have the sixth-best xG per shot (0.14). Algeria will be looking to Riyad Mahrez and Houssem Aouar to provide inspiration in attack.
storylines: Back-to-back games against Alps opposition for Algeria

fixtures.whyWatch: A David v Goliath match between the World Cup debutant and the defending champions. Almost nobody thought Cape Verde will make it through to the group stage, but they fought valiantly in shutting down Spain, coming from behind to earn a point against Uruguay, before nearly winning their game against Saudi Arabia. A Cape Verde win will be the ultimate upset, but they know they have won the support of all the neutrals.
storylines: Defending champions
World Cup debutants
Underdog story
Vozinha v Messi

fixtures.whyWatch: Colombia emerged from their group unbeaten and are certainly a team to keep an eye on. Their next opponent will not be a cakewalk, Ghana qualified to the knockout stage on the back of their strong defense, conceding only two goals. Their 0.07 xGA (Expected Goals Against) per shot, is joint third-best in the tournament so far.
storylines: Dark horse candidate in Colombia
Ghana have a strong defense

fixtures.whyWatch: In Mo Salah, Egypt will always have a decisive chance to win a game. They will fancy their chances against Australia, who also should fancy their chance against Egypt. The Socceroos conceded an alarming number of shots in the group stage (second-highest among teams that made the knockout stage), which will be music to Salah's ears.
storylines: How far can Mo Salah go?
Evenly matched teams
Australia's defense is not the strongers
